name: Orwell Route and Las Tres Huegas Trenches Loop
name_original: Trincheras de Lomas de Orwell y Las Tres Huegas — ida y vuelta desde Pedriguera
description: Cycle a moderate 21.4-mile route from Pedriguera, exploring historical Civil War trenches and the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Perdiguera are situated in a diverse geographical area within the province of Zaragoza, Aragon, Spain. The region features varied terrain including forests, extensive cultivated fields, and steppes, which undergo seasonal changes. To the east, the Sierra de Alcubierre mountains offer distinctive landscapes. The area also includes torrents and natural pools, providing unique microclimates for exploration. Best touring cycling routes around Perdiguera The most popular touring cycling route is Orwell Route and Las Tres Huegas Trenches Loop, a 21.4 miles (34.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 16 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores historical sites and varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Orwell Route Trail, a moderate 26.5 miles (42.6 km) path. This route offers expansive views across the region's cultivated fields and natural steppes. Local touring cyclists also love the Valdeatalaya loop from Villamayor de Gállego, a 10.8 miles (17.3 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 1 hour 41 minutes. Touring cycling around Perdiguera is defined by the Sierra de Alcubierre, diverse forests, and open cultivated fields. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes. The routes in Perdiguera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 5 reviews. More than 130 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Perdiguera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Perdiguera?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes around Perdiguera, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes traverse diverse landscapes, from cultivated fields to the foothills of the Sierra de Alcubierre.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Perdiguera?
A: You can expect a diverse range of terrain, including extensive cultivated fields, forests, and steppes. The region is also characterized by torrents and natural pools, offering unique microclimates. To the east, the Sierra de Alcubierre mountains provide more varied and sometimes challenging landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Perdiguera?
A: The landscapes around Perdiguera undergo seasonal transformations, offering different experiences throughout the year. Spring brings green pastures, while summer features golden fields. The semi-arid continental climate means conditions can vary, with winter sometimes bringing fog that adds a unique atmosphere. For comfortable cycling, spring and autumn generally offer milder temperatures.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Perdiguera?
A: Yes, Perdiguera offers easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31143179" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdeatalaya loop from Villamayor de Gállego&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that leads through the local countryside, providing a good option for those seeking a less strenuous ride.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, there are several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20152285" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Caprasio Trail – San Caprasio Viewpoint loop from Perdiguera&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding option, covering a significant distance with considerable elevation gain. Another challenging route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31499053" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orwell Trenches – Orwell Hill loop from Leciñena&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes in Perdiguera?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Perdiguera are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993096816/orwell-route-and-las-tres-huegas-trenches-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orwell Route and Las Tres Huegas Trenches Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which explores historical sites and varied landscapes, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31143179" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdeatalaya loop from Villamayor de Gállego&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling around Perdiguera?
A: While cycling, you can explore various natural and historical landmarks. Notable natural features include the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2856000" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sabina de Villamayor&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5624174" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pino Guitón&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2963147" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campillo Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5753138" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enebro Peak&lt;/a&gt;, offering expansive views of the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any historical sites to explore on a touring cycling trip?
A: Yes, Perdiguera and its surroundings offer historical points of interest. The town itself features historical buildings such as the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption and the Hermitage of San José, known for its Baroque plasterwork with Mudejar influences. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993096816/orwell-route-and-las-tres-huegas-trenches-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orwell Route and Las Tres Huegas Trenches Loop&lt;/a&gt;, specifically explore historical sites like the Las Tres Huegas Trenches.

Q: Is public transport available to reach cycling routes in Perdiguera?
A: While Perdiguera is a small municipality, it is accessible from larger cities like Zaragoza. Public transport options to the town itself may be limited, so it's advisable to check local bus schedules if you plan to rely on public transport to reach your starting point. Many cyclists prefer to drive to the area.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a touring cycling route in Perdiguera?
A: In Perdiguera, you can typically find parking within the town, especially near the main square or local amenities. For routes starting outside the immediate town center, look for designated parking areas or suitable roadside spots near trailheads, ensuring you do not obstruct local traffic or private property.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly touring cycling routes in Perdiguera?
A: Many of the natural trails and paths around Perdiguera are suitable for cycling with dogs, provided they are well-behaved and kept under control, especially when passing through cultivated fields or near livestock. Always ensure your dog is on a leash where required and respect local regulations regarding pets in natural areas.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Perdiguera?
A: The touring cycling routes in Perdiguera are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Reviewers often praise the varied terrain, the tranquility of the less-trafficked trails like the Camino de Perdiguera, and the expansive views across the region's cultivated fields and natural steppes.

Q: Are there options for longer touring cycling adventures?
A: Yes, for those looking for longer rides, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974058980/orwell-route-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orwell Route Trail&lt;/a&gt; offer a substantial distance of over 40 km, providing expansive views across the region. Combining multiple routes or exploring the extended network of trails accessible from Perdiguera can also create longer adventures.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the "Trincheras de Lomas de Orwell y Las Tres Huegas" route?
faqs.answer: The route conveniently starts next to a parking lot in Pedriguera, making it easy to begin your touring bicycle adventure.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily paved, with approximately 25.9 km of asphalt and 6.36 km of other paved surfaces. There are also about 2.26 km of unpaved sections, which might be more challenging for touring bikes not equipped for off-road use. You'll cycle on state roads (25.1 km), streets (5.58 km), and paths (2.30 km).

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for all fitness levels?
faqs.answer: The overall difficulty is moderate. For the full 34.5 km route with 413 meters of elevation gain, good fitness is required. Shorter versions of the route, up to 2 hours and 150 meters of elevation gain, are suitable for all skill levels. Very good fitness is needed for rides exceeding 5 hours or 450 meters of elevation gain, as some sections might require pushing your bike.

faqs.question: What historical sites and landmarks will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several significant points of interest. In Pedriguera, you can see the picturesque Church of Our Lady of the Assumption of Pedriguera. The route also takes you past the Las Tres Huegas - Civil War Trenches and the Orwell Trenches, offering a glimpse into historical events.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is outdoors, specific regulations for dogs on this particular trail are not detailed. As a general rule for touring bicycle routes, dogs are often allowed if well-behaved and kept on a leash, especially in populated areas or near historical sites. Always ensure your dog is comfortable with the distance and terrain, and carry enough water for them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific seasonal considerations for this route are not provided, similar cycling routes in the region are often best enjoyed during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring cooler, potentially wet conditions. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route, especially within Sierra de Alcubierre?
faqs.answer: This route passes through parts of the Sierra de Alcubierre. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public trails within natural parks like Sierra de Alcubierre. However, always respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route utilizes sections of several other paths. You will find yourself on parts of the Sierra de Alcubierre, Calle del Buen Aire, Camino Loma Orwell, Paseo de Santa Engracia, Paseo de la Santa Cruz, Paseo del Manantial, Camino de la Colladeta de Enmedio, and Camino de las Cambrinas.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, pubs, or accommodation options near Pedriguera for cyclists?
faqs.answer: Pedriguera is a charming town, and while specific cyclist-friendly establishments are not detailed, you can typically find local cafes, pubs, and accommodation options in and around the starting point. It's advisable to check local listings or ask residents upon arrival for the best recommendations.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle ride on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water, snacks, a basic bike repair kit (including a spare tube and pump), appropriate cycling attire, sun protection, and a charged phone. A helmet is always recommended for safety. Consider a GPS device or a map for navigation, though the route is well-defined.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of the 'Trincheras de Lomas de Orwell' and 'Las Tres Huegas'?
faqs.answer: Both 'Trincheras de Lomas de Orwell' (Orwell Trenches) and 'Las Tres Huegas' (The Three Gaps) refer to Civil War Trenches. These sites are remnants of the Spanish Civil War, offering a poignant historical context to your ride. They represent defensive lines and strategic points from that period, providing a unique historical experience along the route.
